home.title: TradeAutomation Research Dashboards
home.intro: Versioned static dashboards for LP + Force Strike experiments. Use stable version pages for comparison instead of overwriting old analysis.

current_baseline.title: Current Baseline: fixed 6-bar pullback wait
current_baseline.description: Use the 0.5 signal-candle pullback, full Force Strike structure stop, single 1R target, and fixed 6-bar pullback wait as the active research baseline.

pages.title: V1 Baseline: Broad Permutation Test
pages.question: Which first-pass entry and exit families are worth studying after combining LP breaks with raw Force Strike signals?
pages.setup: Broad permutation test across M30, H4, D1, and W1 using next-open and midpoint-pullback entries, structure stops, ATR width filters, and fixed R targets.
pages.how_to_read: Treat this as exploration. M30 dominates the signal count, so compare timeframes before reading the aggregate leaderboard.
pages.conclusion: Midpoint-pullback entries were materially better than next-open entries. M30 was weak, while H4, D1, and W1 justified a focused follow-up.
pages.action: Use only as historical context. V2 and later pages contain the refined research path.

pages.title: V2 Focus: H4/D1/W1 Midpoint Entry
pages.question: Does the midpoint-pullback family still work when M30 is removed and ATR risk-width filters are compared?
pages.setup: Focused H4, D1, and W1 test using midpoint-pullback entries, Force Strike structure stops, ATR max-risk variants, and fixed R targets.
pages.how_to_read: Use the focused timeframe tables and ignore M30 because it is not part of this run.
pages.conclusion: The midpoint-pullback structure-stop family stayed positive across H4, D1, and W1. The 1ATR and 1.25ATR variants were close.
pages.action: Use V2 to understand why V3 moved into entry-zone and partial-exit testing.

pages.title: V3 Entry/Exit: Zones, ATRs, Partial Exits
pages.question: Which signal-candle entry zone, ATR risk filter, and exit structure is strongest before stability testing?
pages.setup: H4, D1, and W1 test of 0.5, 0.6, and 0.7 signal-candle pullbacks, ATR max-risk filters, single-target exits, and MT5-portable partial exits.
pages.how_to_read: Compare model families first, then check per-timeframe robustness. This run has many simulated candidate trades because each signal is tested against many permutations.
pages.conclusion: The 0.5 signal-candle pullback was strongest. Single-target 1R beat the tested partial-exit variants for the best individual candidate.
pages.action: Use the 0.5 zone, structure stop, 1R family as the base candidate for later tests.

pages.title: V4 Stability: Train/Test Symbol Filters
pages.question: Can train-period symbol/timeframe filters improve the later test period for the best V3 candidates?
pages.setup: Walk-forward stability test using V3 trades, a 2023 split, and several training-period filters for symbol/timeframe inclusion.
pages.how_to_read: Read the test period first. A filter is useful only if it improves later unseen performance, not just training results.
pages.conclusion: The learned symbol/timeframe filters did not improve the test period. The unfiltered baseline remained better.
pages.action: Do not add symbol/timeframe filters yet. Keep the weak pockets visible but avoid this filtering method.

pages.title: V5 H8 Bridge: Native MT5 H8 Test
pages.question: Does native MT5 H8 provide a useful bridge between H4 and D1?
pages.setup: Single candidate test using 0.5 signal-candle pullback, full Force Strike structure stop, 1R target, and native MT5 H8 data.
pages.how_to_read: Compare H8 against H4 and D1. The aggregate is less important than the quality step between timeframes.
pages.conclusion: H8 improved modestly over H4 but did not become a clean midpoint between H4 and D1.
pages.action: Keep H8 available for comparison, but do not pivot the main strategy research around H8.

pages.title: V6 H12 Bridge: Native MT5 H12 Test
pages.question: Does native MT5 H12 provide a better bridge between H8 and D1?
pages.setup: Single candidate test using 0.5 signal-candle pullback, full Force Strike structure stop, 1R target, and native MT5 H12 data.
pages.how_to_read: Use this as the current timeframe comparison snapshot. Compare H4, H8, H12, D1, and W1 independently.
pages.conclusion: H12 is a meaningful bridge. It is stronger than H8 and closer to D1 quality while keeping more trade count than D1.
pages.action: Keep H12 in the forward research set. Use V6 as the current timeframe snapshot, not V7 or V8.

pages.title: V7 Entry Wait: Cancel At Would-Be 1R
pages.question: Should a pullback order stay open until entry or be cancelled once the would-be 1R target is reached first?
pages.setup: Same 0.5 pullback, structure stop, 1R target model, but pending entry waits until either the pullback fills or the would-be 1R target is reached. If both happen in one candle, V7 cancels first.
pages.how_to_read: This is not a new baseline. It is a rule challenge against the fixed 6-bar pullback wait.
pages.conclusion: Do not replace the fixed 6-bar pullback wait. V7 is unusable because the cancel-first same-bar rule removes too many valid trades.
pages.action: Do not use V7 for strategy selection. Keep the fixed 6-bar entry wait.

pages.title: V8 Entry Wait: Entry-Priority 1R Cancel
pages.question: If a candle touches both pullback entry and would-be 1R, does entry-first handling make the 1R-cancel wait rule usable?
pages.setup: Same test as V7, but if entry and would-be 1R occur in the same candle, the setup fills first instead of cancelling first.
pages.how_to_read: V8 is the fairer version of the 1R-cancel wait rule, but it must beat the fixed 6-bar baseline to replace it.
pages.conclusion: Do not replace the fixed 6-bar pullback wait. V8 is positive but weaker than the baseline on trade count, win rate, average R, PF, and total R.
pages.action: Do not use V8 as the active baseline. Keep the fixed 6-bar entry wait and use V6 for timeframe comparison.
